So as we've shown you in the various tutorials, there's lots and lots of different stats that you can be keeping an eye on, but these are probably the most important. So your cost per conversion. So let's say you're focused on leads and you know, hey, if I can get a lead for 20 bucks, I can be profitable. Then you're going to really want to keep an eye on this. And a big part of this is going to be your page performance. Also, you've got your ROAS return on ad spend. So how much are you making back? Now, of course, you can only track ROAS. If you've told Google how much a conversion is worth. So you can do this in several ways and you'll see this as you're setting up your conversions. It'll ask, Hey, what's the value of one of these conversions? Or if you want to be fancy, you can do it programmatically and have data sent back, but I would just keep it simple out of the gate. So this return on ad spend, obviously very important cost per click. If you're keeping an eye on cost per click, you know, it's not as important as your cost per conversion because maybe you're targeting a higher quality audience and you don't mind paying for the click. But it's a very good indicator to watch your click through rate. If you're seeing you have a low click through rate, then obviously your ad quality is low or you need to refine your call to action. CPV or CPM. These are terms that are varied depending on the kind of ad you're running. CPV is cost per view for video views. CPM is cost per thousand exposures. If you're doing search campaigns or you're doing Google Display Network. But this is just really reflects the overall quality of the campaign. And then if you're doing specifically video ads, your watch time on your video is important. So if you see like, man, nobody's watching the video. So there's many, many more metrics that you can watch. But if you just want to really focus in on these, you can't go wrong. So check those constantly. And then when it comes to managing your actual ads, your bids and your budgeting, right? How much am I spending? That's such an important question. So you're always going to review your ads, your ad groups and your campaigns. So the reason that we put it in this order is that obviously the campaigns are the highest level. And you're going to see your campaigns first and foremost. But it's important that you also click through on the campaign. So let's just say, for example, you have a campaign that's got three ad groups and each ad group has two ads. Well, it's very possible that you've got one ad that's doing really, really, really well. And all the rest of them are doing really bad. If you were to just pause the campaign without looking at the ads first, then you might miss out on a winner. So just make sure that you're drilling down, that you're looking at everything. Ultimately, there's no exact order. There's no exact science. You just want to look at everything. So just make sure you're reviewing your ads, your ad groups, your campaigns. You're going to pause all of the clear losers. And it's important that you have significant spend. I think Grant recommended that you want to spend. I think two to three times the amount of what a conversion value is. So, for example, if you sell a product for $1,000 and you've only spent $400, you don't know if it's a loser. Right? You could spend $600 and make two sales. You never know. So the higher the ticket product, the more you got to spend. So just make sure that you've given the ad a chance. But then as soon as you kind of feel, hey, this is a loser, make sure to pause it. Increase any winning campaign. So anything that's making money, you know, as much as you want to maybe double it or triple it, don't do that. Increase it slowly. 10 to 25% can get the job done. And then you can also adjust your target CPAs cost per action. So basically your bids. And just remembering that setting your actual targets is not a necessary thing in Google.
